channel.title: Chapters on Project Notes
channel.description: Recent content in Chapters on Project Notes

item.title: Chapter 1 — The Optimal Morning Routine
item.description:
The Optimal Morning Routine - Andrew Huberman

Sleep is a fundamental practice or part of 24-hours cycle. Where if you don’t get it on consistent basis you are down-regulating your ability to do everything.

“You should try to get good sleep 80% of the time"

1. Practice getting natural light in your eyes within one hour of waking up

Try to get 5 to 10 minutes of sunlight. Can sunlight enhance passion? Yes!!

item.title: Chapter 2 — Mass Psychosis
item.description:
MASS PSYCHOSIS - How an Entire Population Becomes MENTALLY ILL

“The masses have never thirsted after truth; they turn aside from evidence that does not suit their tastes, preferring to embrace error if it seduces them. Whoever attempts to destroy their illusions is always their victim.”
— Gustav Le Bon

Mass psychosis is an epidemic of madness that occurs when a large portion of society loses touch with reality and descends into delusions.

item.title: Chapter 3 — How to Turn Information Into Intelligence
item.description:
How to turn information into intelligence | Barbara Oakley

There are two distinct ways in which we perceive and understand the world. When we initially learn something, we activate the task-positive network and focus on the subject matter. However, as we encounter difficulties and frustrations, we tend to activate the default mode network, which allows our brains to process information in the background. This diffuse mode of thinking often occurs when we take a break and engage in activities unrelated to the task at hand. Surprisingly, when we return to the task after this period of relaxation, we often find that the information suddenly makes sense and seems easier to understand.

item.title: Chapter 4 — Handwriting Boost Brain Connectivity
item.description:
Handwriting, whether in cursive on a touchscreen or traditional pen and paper, activates extensive brain regions, vital for memory and learning. These findings highlight the importance of balancing traditional handwriting instruction with digital literacy in educational settings.

Key Facts:

  1. Handwriting activates more complex brain connectivity than typing, beneficial for learning and memory.
  2. The study used high-density EEGs to measure brain activity, demonstrating the unique cognitive engagement of handwriting.
  3. The results advocate for maintaining handwriting instruction in schools alongside digital literacy.

“We show that when writing by hand, brain connectivity patterns are far more elaborate than type-writing on a keyboard”

item.title: Chapter 5 — Life of Seneca
item.description:
Early Years

Seneca was probably born in 4 BCE, the same year as Jesus in Cordoba, Spain. His father, Seneca the Elder, was a Roman equestrian who wrote books on rhetoric and Roman history. His elder brother Novatus, known as Gallio, was a senator, and his younger brother Mela was the father of poet Lucan. Seneca was raised from a young age in Rome. His tutors included Attalus the Stoic, sotion, and Papirius Fabianus. The boy Seneca so admired Attalus that he “practically laid siege to his classroom.” He was “the first to arrive and the last to leave” and often left with the desire to be poor.

item.title: Chapter 6 — Tips for Training and Improving Cognitive Skills
item.description:
1. Practice Mindfulness

Regular meditation and mindfulness practices can help you clear your mind and focus better, leading to sharper thinking skills.

  1. Stay Curious

Cultivating a sense of curiosity about the world around you. Learn new things and seek out novel experiences. This will help to develop neural pathways that improve cognitive function.

  1. Engage in Brain Exercises

Puzzles, word games, and memory games can all help to improve cognitive function and keep your mind sharp.

item.title: Chapter 7 — Work Harder on Yourself
item.description:
Work Harder on Yourself!!!!!

Why??

  1. Personal Growth
  2. Self Improvement
  3. Unlock Full Potential

Though no one achieves perfection, viewing growth as an ongoing journey focused on becoming the best version of yourself makes the effort incredibly worthwhile.

Areas to focus on improvement efforts

1.) Physical Health: Prioritizing sufficient sleep, regular exercise, a balanced diet, and avoiding unhealthy habits prevents diseases, boosts energy, and sets the foundation for effective consistent actions like walking, drinking more water, or early bedtimes compound over the years.

item.title: Chapter 8 — Suffering
item.description:
Suffering Makes You Beautiful

— Friedrich Nietzsche

Suffering or pain in broad sense, may be an experience of unpleasantness or aversion, possibly associated with of harm or threat of harm in an individual. Suffering is often categorized as physical or mental.

Suffering occurs in the lives of sentient beings in numerous manners, often dramatically. As a result, many fields of human activity are concerned with some aspects of suffering.

There is theories of psychology present that humans sometimes seek out suffering. Many existentialists believe that suffering is necessary to find meaning in our lives. Existential positive psychology is a theory dedicated to exploring relationship between suffering and happiness and the belief that true authentic happiness can only come from experiencing pain and hardships.

item.title: Chapter 9 — Universe
item.description:
The Universe is Expanding

Since the Big Bang, the universe has been expanding at an incredible rate. Scientists estimate that the universe is expanding at a rate of 70 kilometers per second per megaparsec. This means that the distance between two galaxies that are one megaparsec (3.26 million light-years) apart will increase by 70 kilometers each second.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Expansion_of_the_universe

Big Bang Theory

The Big Bang is a cosmological theory that explains the origin of the universe. The Universe was created from a large explosion about 13.8 billion years ago.

item.title: Chapter 10 — Pain of Regret and Pain of Discipline
item.description:
Just do something, do anything!!!

Start with small things, don’t need to complete a goal or set milestones just yet.

Small things such as:

  • Wake up early and make up bed
  • Stop scrolling and put your phone away
  • Stretch and sunbath for at least 10 minutes a day
  • 5-10 pages per day

Once it’s become a daily routine, kick it up and add more habits on top of the current one. Find the momentum first and improve the habits later on.

item.title: Chapter 11 — Utopia and Dystopia
item.description:
Utopia

A Utopia typically describes an imaginary community or society that possesses highly desirable or near-perfect qualities for its members.

The true nature of Utopia remains elusive, as if inviting us to draw closer and ask critical questions.

What makes Utopia so captivating?

It’s the escape from the harsh realities of our own world. We see utopias as antidotes t inequality, conflict, and suffering, offering a glimpse of world where human potential is finally realized.

item.title: Chapter 12 — No Regrets, Move Forwards
item.description:
NO REGRETS. MOVE FORWARD - JOCKO WILLIK

“No Regrets, Move Forwards” — Jocko Willink

“We often make mistakes”

There are many ways to improve, and we all make mistakes. Often, the lessons we need to learn are right in front of us. The most important thing is to recognize that there is so much to learn from school, people, experiences, and life. However, you must process and absorb this information; you need to accept it and open your mind. This openness will allow you to learn and make progress.

item.title: Chapter 13 — 7 Ways to Keep Your Brain Young
item.description:
1. Get mental stimulation

Through research on mice and humans, scientists have found that mentally stimulating activities encourage the formation of new connections between nerve cells and may even help the brain generate new cells, enhance neural “plasticity,” and build functional reserves to protect against future cell loss. Any activity that stimulates the mind should help develop your brain. Read, take courses, and try “brain exercises,” such as crossword puzzles or math problems. Try activities that require both manual skill and mental effort, such as drawing, painting, and other crafts.

item.title: Chapter 14 — Marcus Aurelius
item.description:
Marcus Aurelius - the author of meditations

Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, known simply as Marcus Aurelius, portrays himself in his writings as a mature male, a dedicated statesman, and the ruler of Rome. At the time he penned this straightforward self-description, he occupied the esteemed position of emperor. Born on April 26, AD 121, in Rome as Marcus Annius Verus, he would go on to shape the course of history until his death on March 17, 180 AD, at the northern frontiers of the Roman Empire.

item.title: Chapter 15 — The Struggle for Virtue
item.description:
There are 4:

  1. Prudent Self Control
  2. Practical Intelligence
  3. Courage
  4. Justice

Prudent Self Care

  • Practice Mindfulness: Pay attention to your thoughts and emotions without judgment. This allows you to identify unhealthy patterns and choose how you react.
  • Delay Gratification: Don’t be impulsive. Take time to consider the consequences of your actions before indulging in desires.
  • Set Boundaries: Learn to say no to protect your time and energy from things that don’t align with your values.

Practical Intelligence

  • Seek Knowledge: Read, learn new things, and engage in critical thinking.
  • Consider Different Perspectives: Don’t jump to conclusions. Evaluate situations from all angles before making decisions.
  • Learn from Mistakes: Everyone makes them. See them as opportunities for growth and improvement.

Courage

  • Face your Fears: Don’t avoid challenges, step outside your comfort zone and take calculated risks.
  • Focus on what can you control: Don’t worry too much about things that are beyond your control. Focus your energy on the things you can influence.
  • Develop Resilience: Life has setbacks. Learn to bounce back from difficulties and keep moving forward.

Justice

  • Treat others with fairness and respect: Everyone deserves to be valued, regardless of background or beliefs.
  • Stand up for what’s rights: Speak out against injustice even if it’s uncomfortable.
  • Fulfill your responsibilities: Be honest, reliable, and keep your commitments.

item.title: Chapter 16 — How Our Brain Process Music
item.description:
Music has been central to human cultures for tens of thousands of years, but how our brains perceive it has long been shrouded in mystery.

key facts:

1. Dual Processing for Music: The brain tracks melody pitch using neurons also involved in speech processing and employs a unique set of neurons for predicting musical notes.

2. Unique Neurons for Music: For the first time, researchers identified neurons specifically dedicated to anticipating the sequence of melodic notes, separate from speech processing.

item.title: Chapter 17 — Break up with Your Phones
item.description:
Why We’re Addicted to Phones?

  • Phones are designed to capture and hold our attention using features like notifications, endless scrolling, and gamified elements.
  • Dopamine plays a significant role in keeping us hooked by rewarding our brain for device interactions.

The Impact of Phone Overuse

  • Reduced focus and creativity.
  • Increased anxiety and feelings of inadequacy, often due to social media.
  • Disruption of real-life connections and experiences.

Steps to Break the Addiction

  • Awareness: Understand your habits by tracking screen time and identifying triggers.
  • Boundaries: Set rules for phone use, such as no-phone zones or times.
  • Mindfulness: Replace impulsive scrolling with purposeful actions, like reading or hobbies.

Building a Healthier Relationship

  • Use your phone as a tool, not a constant companion.
  • Turn off unnecessary notifications.
  • Prioritize face-to-face interactions and offline activities.

item.title: Chapter 18 — Distraction is a bad habit!!
item.description:
Distraction is a bad habit!!!

Breaking habit takes time. There will likely be moments when you slip back into old patterns. The key is to identify what triggered the lapse and have a plan to get back on track. Be patient and consistent with your efforts

item.title: Chapter 19 — Boredom Leads to Greatness
item.description:
Boredom Leads to Greatness

Nowadays people avoid boredom when they go to the bathroom or while they eat, always bringing their phone. They are scared of boring things. Well, that is because boredom is uncomfortable and tough.

Boredom can lead us to greatness and give us ideas.

We are less bored than our ancestors were, but we are more afraid of boredom. Boredom is one of the best things that happen to you and something that changed the lives of many individuals, people are so afraid to be alone with our thoughts.

item.title: Chapter 20 — Universe part 2
item.description:
Singularities

A singularity is a point in spacetime where gravity becomes infinitely strong. According to physics, the center of a black hole is a singularity. Gravity is so strong that not even light can escape its pull.

Read More: https://www.livescience.com/what-is-singularity

Space and Time

Our everyday experience of reality consists of three spatial dimensions (length, width, and height) and one temporal dimension (time) According to Einstein’s theory of relativity, space and time are not separate entities but rather interwoven into a single fabric called space-time. The curvature of spacetime is what we experience as gravity.

item.title: Chapter 22 — The Secret to Thought
item.description:
When it comes to impulse control, the mind is our definite test.

If we are not aware, tiny problems can easily open the window to a whole stream of negativity, fear, and self-doubt that our whole world is dark.

Here are a few tricks to resist those demons:

  1. Close the window as soon as it opens:

Whenever negative thoughts enter your mind, neglect them as soon as you become aware and counter them with a positive one. The longer you wait, the more dense that negativity becomes, it builds up and becomes uncontrollable. This is a difficult habit, but it becomes automatic with practice.

item.title: Chapter 23 — I forgot LOL
item.description: Forgot to write chapter 23. That’s it LOL..

item.title: Chapter 24 — Strategies to Overcome Procrastination
item.description:
1. Concentrate on forming habits by taking small steps forward every day.
  1. Just start working. Force yourself to start and to make the first step. The hardest step is always the first step. Once you take the first step you forget about procrastination and the job gets done.
  1. Consider your energy levels. Take regular breaks throughout the day (walk, stretch, eat, water, nap, etc.) find your peak productive hours, and push yourself, when your energy is low take those breaks.
  1. Overcome the fear of failure, the fear of failure can lead to procrastination. Although failure is a key component of success, you can’t have one without the other. If you want to succeed faster, you have to fail more. Learn from those failures and thrive.
  1. Don’t let perfectionism hold you back. Chasing after perfection can lead to procrastination, but understanding that nothing will ever be perfect empowers you to conquer this challenge. By breaking the cycle of perfectionism, you’ll unlock incredible opportunities you never thought possible.
  1. Creating a list can help you feel productive. Checking off items as “done” keeps you motivated and organized. Additionally, making a list decreases the chances of forgetting tasks.

item.title: Chapter 25 — Your Mind is a Fighter
item.description:
Life is a battle for me

Every single day…..

Im fighting with my own lust, thought, and temptation

That’s what I’m going through… no lie.

We are fighting against false impressions. We’re fighting against destructive emotions like greed, fear, envy, and prejudice. We’re fighting against our lower self to reach that one worthy of being cheered and celebrated. it’s a fight that happens day in and day out. One that never really ends.

item.title: Chapter 26 — Coldest Winter
item.description:
Coldest Winter from 808s & Heartbreak Album by Kanye West (one of my favorite track from 808)

“Coldest Winter”—an impassioned farewell to two important women in Kanye’s life—is his rendition of the 1983 song “Memories Fade” by Tears for Fears. The first of the women is his mother, Donda West, who died due to complications from cosmetic surgery in 2007. Donda had been a constant source of love and guidance in addition to taking on some of Kanye’s managerial responsibilities. The second is fashion designer Alexis Phifer with whom West had a six-year relationship (and two-year engagement). His mother’s death and Kanye’s demanding work schedule ultimately laid this romance to rest.

item.title: Chapter 27 — Sunlight
item.description:
Sunlight……

❓How sunshine can enhance your health:

  • Vitamin D Production: Vitamin D plays a starring role in bone health by promoting calcium absorption, which keeps bones strong and reduces the risk of osteoporosis.

  • Mood Magic: Exposure to sunlight is linked to increased production of serotonin, a brain chemical that regulates mood. Higher serotonin levels are associated with feelings of happiness, calmness, and focus.

  • Immune system ally: Adequate vitamin D levels are associated with a stronger immune system, which means our body is better equipped to fight off illness and infections.

item.title: Chapter 28 — Dreams
item.description:
Source: https://www.sleepfoundation.org/dreams

What Are Dreams?

Dreams are images, thoughts, or feelings that occur during sleep. Visual imagery is the most common, but dreams can involve all of the senses. Some people dream in color while others dream in black and white, and people who are blind tend to have more dream components related to sound, taste, and smell.

Characteristics of dreaming include:

  • It has a first-person perspective.
  • It is involuntary.
  • The content may be illogical or even incoherent.
  • The content includes other people who interact with the dreamer and one another.
  • It provokes strong emotions.
  • Elements of waking life are incorporated into content.

Why Do We Dream?

  • Building memory: Dreaming has been associated with the consolidation of memory, which suggests that dreaming may serve an important cognitive function of strengthening memory and informational recall.
  • Processing emotion: The ability to engage with and rehearse feelings in different imagined contexts may be part of the brain’s method for managing emotions.
  • Mental housekeeping: Periods of dreaming could be the brain’s way of “straightening up,” clearing away partial, erroneous, or unnecessary information.
  • Instant replay: Dream content may be a form of distorted instant replay in which recent events are reviewed and analyzed.
  • Incidental brain activity: This view holds that dreaming is just a by-product of sleep that has no essential purpose or meaning.

When Do We Dream?

On average, most people dream for around two hours per night. Dreaming can happen during any stage of sleep, but dreams are the most prolific and intense during the rapid eye movement (REM) stage. During the REM sleep stage, brain activity ramps up considerably compared to the non-REM stages, which helps explain the distinct types of dreaming during these stages. Dreams during REM sleep are typically more vivid, fantastical, and/or bizarre even though they may involve elements of waking life. By contrast, non-REM dreams tend to involve more coherent content that involves thoughts or memories grounded in a specific time and place. REM sleep is not distributed evenly through the night. The majority of REM sleep happens during the second half of a normal sleep period, which means that dreaming tends to be concentrated in the hours before waking up.

item.title: Chapter 29 — Sorry not Sorry
item.description:
I AM SORRY…..

SORRY TO OLD ME.. SORRY TO NEW ME…..

SORRY TO MYSELF

SORRYNOTSORRY

IAMSORRY

this is not a song though

So many mistakes, but I always try to do better…

Song: Sorry not Sorry - Tyler, The Creator

item.title: Chapter 30 — The Less I Know The Better
item.description:
This Chapter About Song

Currents - Tame Impala

The Less I Know The Better It is a psychedelic rock and disco-infused masterpiece that showcases the band’s knack for blending retro and contemporary sounds into an irresistibly catchy tune. Released as part of their critically acclaimed album “Currents” in 2015, the song became of Tame Impala’s most popular and commercially successful tracks.

At its core “The Less I Know The Better” is a tale of love and heartache, exploring themes of desire, jealousy, and longing. The lyrics tell the story of a protagonist who is infatuated with someone who is involved with another person, leading to feelings of frustration and confusion. Despite the emotional turmoil, the song maintains an upbeat and danceable rhythm, creating a juxtaposition between its melancholic lyrics and infectious groove.

item.title: Chapter 31 — 12 Reasons Why Reading Books Should be Part of Your Life
item.description:
1. Knowledge Highway

Books offer a vast source of knowledge on virtually any topic imaginable. Dive deep into history, science, and philosophy, or explore new hobbies and interests.

  1. Enhanced Vocabulary

Regular reading exposes a wide range of vocabulary, improving your communication skills and comprehension.

  1. Memory Boost

Studies suggest that reading can help sharpen your memory and cognitive function, keeping your mind active and engaged.

  1. Stress Reduction

Relaxing while reading good books can be a way to cope with everyday anxiety and a chance to relieve stress.

item.title: Chapter 32 — Why Solitude Promote Greatness
item.description:
Why Solitude Promotes Greatness - The Benefits of Being Alone

The idea is that solitude is not the same as loneliness and can actually bring great benefits. While loneliness is harmful to health, solitude helps in self-development, personal freedom, and mental well-being. Many great thinkers and leaders, such as Jesus, Buddha, Nietzsche, and Thoreau, found inspiration and clarity through solitude. They believed that being alone allows a person to think freely, develop their character, and escape the pressures of society.

item.title: Chapter 33 — Humans Are Terrifying part 0
item.description:
Humans Are Terrifying

Humans may not be the strongest, fastest, or biggest creatures, but what makes them truly terrifying is their endurance and persistence. Ancient humans used a hunting technique called persistence hunting, where they would chase their prey for hours until it collapsed from exhaustion. Unlike other animals, humans can run long distances without overheating because they sweat efficiently. Even when food is scarce, their bodies can survive by burning stored fat, allowing them to keep moving forward. This relentlessness is frightening, as many animals simply give up when they realize they cannot outrun a human.

item.title: Chapter 34 — Humans Are Terrifying part 1
item.description:
Humans Are Terrifying

In the year 2145, Earth has turned into a wasteland, a silent, ruined dystopia brought about by centuries of human greed and destruction. The sky is forever gray, shrouded in a cloud of smog, and the forests that were once lush have withered into skeletal remains. The oceans, now toxic and lifeless, lap at shores littered with the debris of humanity’s golden age. The few remaining cities are fortresses of steel and concrete, towering monoliths serving as grim reminders of human ingenuity turned to evil. Between the ruins and decay, humanity reigns supreme, feared and reviled by every other living creature. The animal kingdom—those who have not gone extinct—has learned to avoid humans at all costs. Humans have become the ultimate predators in their relentless pursuit of progress.

item.title: Chapter 35 — How Genius People got Ideas
item.description:
How geniuses got their ideas:

  • Preparation & Deep Knowledge

Geniuses often spend years immersing themselves in their field. This deep understanding becomes a foundation for their ideas.

  • Connecting the Dots

They’re great at making unusual connections between seemingly unrelated concepts. It’s like looking at puzzle pieces and seeing how they fit together in a new way.

  • Persistence & experimentation

Great ideas rarely appear in a flash of lightning. Geniuses experiment, fail a lot, and refine their ideas over time.

item.title: Chapter 36 — Don't Become Slave to Pleasure
item.description:
Dostoevsky - Don’t Become A Slave to Pleasure

In The Brothers Karamazov, Dostoevsky compares two types of people: Worldly People and Monks. Worldly People chase pleasure and comfort, but in doing so, they lose their freedom. On the other hand, Monks renounce pleasure and, as a result, remain truly free. But why is that the case? According to Dostoevsky, freedom is not just the ability to do whatever you want, but the ability to endure suffering. If you can’t endure suffering, you become a slave to anything that protects you from it. Worldly People believe freedom lies in pleasure, so they dedicate their lives to acquiring wealth, status, luxury, and comfort. However, when these things are taken away, they suffer—and since they don’t know how to handle suffering, they become desperate to escape it, serving anyone who promises to restore their comfort.

item.title: Chapter 37 — Types of Procrastinators
item.description:
1. The Distracted Procrastinator

Typical Behaviors:

  • You don’t respect your time blocks and allow yourself to be distracted.
  • Most of your day consists of minor tasks.
  • You don’t feel 100% present in your work.

Solutions:

  • Block out distractions in your environment.
  • Have a list of Not to-dos.
  • Plan for breaks in your study sessions, incorporate a reward system.

2. The Busy Procrastinator

You’ve committed to too many projects to the point of having no time left.

item.title: Chapter 38 — Secret to Getting Better at Talking to People
item.description:
Secret To Getting Better At Talking To People

How To Do It?

Be genuinely interested:

People love talking about themselves, so ask thoughtful questions and actively listen to their responses.

Listen actively:

Pay attention not just to their words but also their body language.

Open yourself up:

Share some things about yourself too. Conversations are a two-way street, and reciprocity helps build connections.

Nonverbal communication:

Maintain eye contact, smile, and use open body language.

item.title: Chapter 39 — Memento Mori
item.description:
Memento Mori is an artistic or symbolic concept that serves as a reminder of the inevitability of death. This idea has its roots in the philosophies of classical antiquity and Christianity, and it became prominent in funerary art and architecture from the medieval period onward.

What happens after death? The one thing we know for sure is that death is inevitable. The uncertainties around the topic have led to the creation of different art forms which constantly remind us that our life on this earth along with all its delights irrevocably comes to a halt.

item.title: Chapter 40 — The Last Man
item.description: FrAanx is the only conscious human in a world full of delusions. He woke up from a dark, boring, and empty dream, only to realize that the differences in his mind were merely tricks. All this time, FrAanx felt that his left side was just a shadow of his true reality. With this new awareness, FrAanx decided to escape from a place where other humans were still trapped in unconsciousness.
